title: Bastion proxy rewrite causes split terminal fragments and missing final sentences on SSE resume
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer (FinLedger) reports intermittent loss of the final sentence for long SSE streams when an upstream bastion performs a short connection reset and header rewrite. The terminal JSON chunk often appears split across the reset boundary and the client receives an incomplete closing fragment (missing end_event / closing bracket).

Impact:
- ~0.6% of long (>20s) streaming responses truncated during high load windows, causing client-side retries and duplicate prefixes.
- Affected flows: interactive chat completions and long-form document generation.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool in Redwood hosted prod (us-east-1). Model pinned: redwood-3b-chat v2026-02-28.
- Customer uses a managed bastion that rewrites headers and proxies HTTP/1.1 <-> HTTP/2. Bastion performs idle probes and occasionally injects a content-length header when replaying buffered data.

Steps attempted to reproduce:
1) FinLedger test harness: generate a 40s streaming chat with a multi-sentence closing paragraph.
2) Simulate bastion idle-probe which issues a short TCP RST upstream after 30s and re-establishes the connection. During replay the bastion injects Content-Length and adjusts a custom X-Trace header.
3) Capture SSE stream on client and server sides and compare sequence numbers and JSON boundaries.

Observed behavior:
- The final JSON token sequence is split so the parser receives a trailing fragment like: '...
    "choices": [ { "delta": { "content": "...and that's all.'
' then a reset; after reconnect the resume contains the remainder 'final" } ] }' which results in either an unclosed JSON array or duplicated system/user tokens depending on client retry logic.
- Server-side logs show the last write completed before connection tear but the edge-proxy reports a midstream rewrite and replay.
- FinLedger clients report missing concluding sentences and increased retry loops.

Investigation notes:
- Edge-proxy (customer bastion) traces show idle-probe RST at 30s followed by a buffered replay that inserts Content-Length and rewrites X-Forwarded-For and a proprietary X-BSTN-ID header.
- Redwood gateway logs correlate the timeline: upstream half-close -> connection rebind -> resumed SSE frames with slightly shifted byte offsets.
- Hypothesis: header-injection during replay triggers downstream HTTP/2 framing that re-chunks the buffered bytes, splitting JSON chunk boundaries and removing SSE end_event marker under certain buffer alignments.
- Reproducible when the final chunk size is > 1KB and the bastion replay path engages header normalization. Not reproduced with small final chunks.

Actions taken so far:
- Support ingested FinLedger sample traces and captured both client and server pcap and request/response traces.
- SRE ran an isolated repro in staging with the customer bastion simulator to confirm split occurs when proxy injects content-length.
- Created github PR to add defensive reassembly logic in edge-proxy shim (PR referenced above).
- Temporarily proposed customer workaround: disable bastion header-rewrite during long streaming sessions or enable aggressive keepalive to avoid the idle-probe reset.

Next steps / mitigation:
- Short-term: recommend FinLedger disable content-length injection for streaming endpoints and apply keepalive tuning on bastion. CS notified customer and provided rollback steps.
- Medium-term: SRE/edge team to land PR#4126 which adds a buffered-sse reassembler and preserves SSE event boundaries across replay. Targeted rollout to staging by 2026-03-14 and prod canary 2026-03-17.
- Long-term: add heuristics in gateway to detect partial JSON terminal fragments and trigger an automatic reassembly/fallback policy instead of surface replay that can split tokens.

Customer communication log:
- 2026-03-10 09:12 (Ava Chen -> FinLedger): acknowledged, requested sample traces and customer bastion config.
- 2026-03-11 13:04 (FinLedger Senior Eng): provided pcap, bastion config (idle_probe=30s), and production examples.
- 2026-03-12 16:20 (Diego Ramos -> FinLedger): suggested temporary workaround and confirmed SRE is reproducing in staging.

Resolution notes (pending):
- Workaround applied by customer reduced truncation rate to <0.01% during a 6-hour observation window.
- Engineering to merge PR and monitor canary; ticket remains In Progress until fix deployed and verified.

Attachments / traces: provided as links in support portal and referenced in PR comments.

title: Variant redispatch to secondary model during staged canary due to orchestrator snapshot lag
preview:
Issue summary: During a staged canary rollout for redwood-llama-7b-v2 in us-east, ~3% of BrightHealth AI traffic was unexpectedly routed to the secondary variant (quantized fallback) despite an explicit tenant model pin. Impact: customer reported mixed responses in production affecting 12 tenant sessions (chat flows). Symptoms observed at 09:21 UTC: inconsistent model outputs from the same session-id, and increased token latency (p50 -> p95).

High-level hypothesis: orchestrator snapshot application lag caused a partial control-plane commit where edge proxies had stale routing snapshots while the central router had already committed a canary weight update. This led to a redispatch path where requests that should have be pinned were re-evaluated by a reconciliation step and dispatched to the secondary variant.

Requested outcome: restore full pin coherence for affected tenant(s), identify root cause in orchestration snapshot propagation, and add guardrails to avoid redispatch during staged commits.

title: Private: scheduled-worker service tokens receive 403 after STS realm claim contains slash
preview:
Issue summary:
NovaAnalytics reports that scheduled background workers began failing with 403s when calling Redwood Private admin APIs after their internal STS rotation. Impact: dozens of scheduled jobs failing across environments, customer unable to run nightly ingestion.

Impact:
- Customer production scheduled workers returning 403 on admin/model endpoints.
- Jobs retried, causing backlog and higher cost.
- Affects all workers that use service tokens issued by customer's STS.

Environment:
- Redwood Private (on-prem bundle v1.6.2) deployed in NovaAnalytics datacenter (prod-us-east).
- Identity stack: internal STS -> ingress-proxy -> redwood identity-proxy -> private control plane.
- Auth scheme: bearer token with JWT including custom "realm" claim used by customer's RBAC mapping.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Obtain service token from customer's STS for job-runner service account (token contains claim: "realm": "edge/analytics").
2) POST to /v1/admin/models with Authorization: Bearer <token>.
3) Observe 403 response.

Observed behavior:
- Redwood returns 403 with message: "access denied: no matching role bindings for principal".
- Identity-proxy logs show claim parsing step: logged claim value truncated to "edge", roles not resolved.
- Customer's STS started emitting realm values with forward-slash (/) as part of namespace after recent rotation. Previously used hyphens only.

Expected behavior:
- RBAC claim parser should accept arbitrary UTF-8 in realm claim and treat it atomically (not split on '/'), or at a minimum provide configurable claim delimiter rules.

Relevant logs (redacted):
- identity-proxy: "parsed_claims={\"sub\":\"svc-jobrunner\",\"realm\":\"edge\"}"
- auth-service: "role_bindings_lookup: principal=svc-jobrunner@edge -> bindings=[]"
- customer-proxy-ingress: "received original-claim: realm=edge/analytics"

Temporary workaround provided:
- Instructed customer to update token transformation rule on their ingress-proxy to URL-encode '/' in realm claims (produces realm=edge%2Fanalytics). This allows Redwood parser to treat it as single token.

Customer notes:
- Change was low-impact and applied successfully; jobs resumed within ~20 minutes after proxy change.

Work requested from Redwood:
- Patch to identity-proxy/runtime to avoid splitting on '/' and to accept raw claim strings for role lookup OR documentation on claim normalization behavior and recommended proxy transforms.

title: Console alert duplication and incorrect attribution after hourly retention compaction
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer KappaHealth reported multiple duplicate alert notifications for the same console rule over a four-hour window after our hourly retention compaction job ran. Alerts were emitted with different attribution metadata (org/route/model) and caused duplicated paging to their on-call.

Impact:
- Several duplicate P1 alerts sent to KappaHealth on-call (SMS + PagerDuty) between 02:00–06:00 UTC on 2026-03-10.
- Noise led to escalation and temporary disruption of their incident response.
- Affects alert evaluation and attribution reports in Console for orgs with high-cardinality tags.

Environment:
- Production (us-east-1), console and alerting clusters behind ALB.
- Retention compaction job v2.3.1 (daily/hourly rollups) ran at 02:00 UTC.

Steps to reproduce (internal):
1) On a high-cardinality org with many tag permutations, enable a per-route 5m error-rate alert with 1m evaluation window.
2) Allow ingest and alerting to run across an hourly retention compaction boundary.
3) Observe alert eval log entries and notification dispatch records for duplicate IDs with differing attribution fields.

Observed behavior:
- Two or more alerts created for the same condition within a short span after compaction completes.
- Alert objects have different attribution fields (route_id vs. fallback_route) and different timebucket keys, causing duplicate evaluation paths.

Expected behavior:
- Single alert evaluation result per incident, with stable attribution preserved across retention compaction.

Initial workaround offered to customer:
- We suggested temporarily disabling hourly compaction for their org (not recommended long-term) and narrow the alert to use a route_id whitelist to ensure single attribution.
- Advised they mute duplicate PagerDuty escalations until we provide a permanent fix.

title: Cascading API-key handovers causing tenant concurrency exhaustion and intermittent 429 bursts
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports a burst of 429 responses across their tenant after an automated API key rotation during a high-throughput streaming upload window. The pattern is reproducible when their client rotates keys and simultaneously spawns short-lived prefetch workers that fan out requests to multiple routes.

Impact:
- LexaHealth observed ~30% request failures (429) for 7–12 minutes after an automated key rotation during a nightly data push.
- Business impact: ingestion pipeline stalled; downstream throughput SLA missed.

Steps to reproduce (provided by customer):
1) Configure two API keys (K-A primary, K-B rotated-in) with equal privileges under same tenant.
2) Client spawns 40 short-lived prefetch workers that request streaming completions and embeddings concurrently (burst over ~10s).
3) Client triggers automated rotation: start sending requests with both K-A and K-B for an overlap period of ~30s.
4) Observe 429s returned to many workers within 1–2 minutes of rotation, continuing for several minutes.

Observed behavior:
- 429 responses show rate-limiter reason in headers: X-RateLimit-Reason: concurrency_window_exhausted
- Token-bucket headers show remaining=0 for tenant-level bucket while per-key buckets occasionally show small positive values.
- Logs include many short-lived connection opens/terminates tied to rotated key handoff.

Expected behavior:
- Smooth handover with exponential backoff from clients should avoid tenant-level token exhaustion; short overlap should not trigger wide 429 storms.

Customer-provided artifacts:
- Upload link to request traces (redacted): s3://leixa-logs/rotation-2026-03-09/traces.tar.gz
- Curl examples and client pseudocode for rotation logic included in attachments.

Immediate ask:
- Guidance on mitigation steps to stop ongoing 429 bursts and recommended configuration changes for dedicated customers performing frequent key rotations.

title: Reserved pool throughput degradation caused by GPU power-capping + fragmented micro-batching
preview: Issue summary: Customer BeaconAI observed sustained p95/p99 latency regressions and intermittent 504 timeouts during peak windows on their dedicated reserved pool. Impact: Customer reports request timeouts and model responses taking 2x-4x expected time; production product is degrading for end users. Timeline: problem first reported 2026-03-10 08:10 UTC, support triaged and opened this ticket. Initial hypothesis included noisy-tenant and autoscaler undershoot. This ticket captures investigation showing a combination of node-level GPU power capping (thermal/clock throttling) combined with workload micro-batching fragmentation and a conservative autoscaler min_replicas setting.

title: Intermittent timeouts and doubled latency for repeated prompts: stale prefix / KV cache misses during streamed retry path
preview:
Issue summary: Customer (AtlasWrite, Dedicated) reports intermittent per-request latency spikes (1.8-2.5x baseline) and occasional streaming timeouts when users resubmit identical prompts or when client SDK retries a broken stream. Impact: Several customer-facing flows (autosummarize & chat history replay) experience user-facing slowdowns and ~5-10% request failures during peak traffic windows. Environment: prod/us-east, dedicated pool id d-pool-12. Observed primarily on gptx-13b-quantized and gptx-8b-finetune. Steps to reproduce: 1) Send a multi-turn conversation with a repeated prompt token prefix. 2) Force a transient stream disconnect on the client to trigger retry logic (SDK exponential backoff). 3) Observe server-side logs and latency metrics for repeated prompt ID. Expected behavior: Repeat prompt should hit warmed prefix/KV cache and return near-baseline latency with streaming retry preserving KV cache. Actual behavior: First retry shows KV cache miss, full KV lookup or cold fetch (~150-300ms extra) and in some cases the retry path times out after streaming proxy backpressure, producing a 504 to the caller. Recent change: Queryable prefix indexing refactor (serving-runtime v2.8.0) rolled 2026-03-08 suspected to coincide with regression window. Attachments: customer traces, sample request headers, and timing graphs uploaded to ticket attachments.

Logs / snippets: Error patterns observed in logs (prod us-east): "kv-cache: prefix-miss for key=prefix_0x4f2a" followed by "kv-fetch: cross-pool fetch latency=210ms" and then "streaming-proxy: retry-forward - client-side disconnect, initiating retry path". Several entries show the serving node evicting prefix entries after background compaction even when traffic indicates hot prefixes.

title: Blended invoice missing per-workload rate memo for mixed streaming+batch usage
preview: Issue summary: Customer BrightScale.ai received March invoice with a single blended line item labeled 'multi-workload blended rate' but there is no memo breakdown showing how streaming vs batch embeddings usage were apportioned or which rate tiers applied. Impact: customer cannot reconcile costs against internal chargebacks and is requesting detailed memo lines or provisional credit. Environment: production billing run for billing period 2026-02-01 to 2026-02-28 covering hosted and dedicated fallbacks across us-east and eu-west. Customer says invoice total matches expectation but line-item explanation is insufficient for their finance team.

title: Mid-cycle per-model rounding caused multi-line invoice delta and provisional credit hold
preview:
Issue summary: Customer reporting a $4,312.57 discrepancy across three invoice line items after they changed reserved capacity and model variant pins mid-billing-window. Impact: Customer billing shows duplicate-looking microbatch line items with fractional token rounding that sum to an unexpected delta. Provisional credit that normally applies during postback reconciliation was withheld pending investigation.

Context: Novus Retail migrated a portion of traffic from mixlm-13b-v1 to redwood/quantized-medium-7b on 2026-03-08 at ~18:40 UTC to reduce cost. They are on a Dedicated contract with hourly reserved capacity. The pricing tier change happened roughly in the middle of the hourly accumulation window which led to blended per-token rates for the same API key. The customer expects the platform to attribute usage to a single effective tier and apply smoothing/rounding consistently; instead the invoice contains three separate lines for similar microbatch events and a withheld provisional credit labeled "auto-reconcile-hold".

title: Edge failover + auth rotation causes missing metadata envelope and trailing paragraph silence on streaming resume
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports intermittent incomplete final metadata and a silent trailing paragraph when their websocket or SSE stream resumes after an edge failover/auth cookie rotation. The response body contains the content tokens but the final metadata envelope (terminal JSON object containing stream-level fields like 'id'/'usage'/'choices' metadata) is sometimes missing or cut and the last paragraph is not emitted, causing the client SDK to throw a parse error or appear to hang.

Impact:
- Several Inkwell Media users see a parse error or a UI spinner after a reconnect; last paragraph (summary) often never appears.
- Occurs ~0.5-1% of long-running streaming sessions for this customer during peak traffic.
- Affects critical editor flows where the final paragraph contains a license blurb.

Environment:
- Customer: Inkwell Media (dedicated account)
- Region: us-east
- Model: redwood-3b-v1 pinned
- Path: Streaming via websocket upgrade (wss) through edge-proxy cluster with header rewrite and cookie rotation set by customer-auth service
- Client: Browser webapp using Redwood JS SDK v1.4.2 behind corporate NAT/proxy

Steps to reproduce (attempted):
1) Start long-form streaming generation via websocket from the customer app with conversation payload > 800 tokens.
2) While streaming, trigger an auth cookie rotation (customer does so via SSO refresh) or simulate an edge failover by cycling one of the edge-proxy instances.
3) Client reconnects and requests resume using the session token; streaming continues but the final metadata object is missing or truncated and the closing paragraph is not emitted.

Observed behavior:
- Streaming tokens continue to arrive, and the last content tokens look complete, but no terminal metadata envelope (closing JSON with 'id' and aggregated usage) is present.

title: Dedicated pool throughput collapse from connection-reservoir leak causing batched-slot starvation
preview:
Issue summary:
DataScribe reported a sudden drop in dedicated pool throughput in us-west-2 starting 2026-03-10 14:10 UTC. Customer traffic experienced elevated p50/p95/p99 for batched routes; many short requests were queued and not scheduled to GPUs.

Impact:
- Customer-facing latency p99 increased from ~220ms to >2.8s for batched generation routes.
- Throughput dropped ~40% for the reserved pool attached to DataScribe's dedicated capacity.
- Automatic fallback to lower-tier variants triggered repeatedly, causing billing and quality regressions.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool: ds-prod-reserved-17 (us-west-2)
- Serving runtime: 2026.02.21-release
- Autoscaler: adaptive-v3
- Affected models: redwood-v1-13b (pinned), redwood-v2-7b (fallback)

Steps to reproduce (from customer):
1) Run high concurrency burst of many short chatlet streams (avg 120 tokens, 50ms client think-time) pinned to ds-prod-reserved-17.
2) Observe steady-state: throughput falls after ~20 minutes of sustained small-stream traffic; p99 climbs.

Observed behavior:
- Long-lived TCP connection handles and scheduler slots remained reserved even when requests idle between tokens.
- Scheduler shows high "connection_reservoir_in_use" metric approaching pool capacity; batchers unable to form optimal batches because slots appear held by idle connections.
- Autoscaler did not provision additional GPUs due to perceived capacity fullness; eviction signals low.

Expected behavior:
- Idle connections should yield reservoir slots after short idle window so batchers can reassign slots to active requests.

Relevant logs and dashboards:
- Loki query: {job=\"serving-scheduler\",pool=\"ds-prod-reserved-17\"} |~ \"reservoir\"
- Grafana dashboard: /d/pool/ds-prod-reserved-17/throughput-latency
- Trace sample: trace.redwood.ai/trace/8a3f2e7b

title: Erratic 5xx surge correlating with prefetch stall + connection-throttling on API gateway
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer (CloudNote Labs, dedicated) started seeing a sudden increase in 5xx responses for text-generation API calls beginning ~2026-03-10 01:18 UTC. Errors are intermittent but sustained in bursts over 10-30 minute windows.

Impact:
- ~18% of requests from the customer's endpoint returned 5xx during bursts, affecting production feature serving (user-facing).
- Visible increase in tail latency for streaming sessions.

Environment:
- Hosted: Dedicated pool for CloudNote (us-east region).
- Models: redwood/open-llama-7b pinned -> gptq-6b fallback configured.
- Traffic pattern: periodic prefetch-heavy workload (long prompts + multi-call prefetch).

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1) Run a sustained throughput test with prefetch_enabled=true and keep-alive connections from ~100 concurrent clients.
2) Observe API gateway connection throttling counters spike and upstream runtime 5xx rates rise.
3) Some streaming sessions drop with 502/503; non-streaming requests see 500/504.

Observed behavior:
- API gateway shows 'conn_throttle' metric jumps correlated to 5xx bursts.
- Runtime logs show short-lived worker exits with OOM/EAGER-EVICT warnings around same timestamps.
- KV-prefetch subsystem reports elevated cache eviction/repoll events (kv_prefetch_miss_rate).
- Autoscaler shows cooldown windows preventing immediate scale-up during initial burst.

Expected behavior:
- Stable 2xx success rate with graceful autoscaler scaling, no connection-level throttling for dedicated customers within committed capacity.

title: Streaming tool/function output tokens billed on final invoice despite console estimate showing zero
preview:
Issue summary: Customer reports that streaming requests which include function/tool calls produced console estimates showing 0 tool-output tokens (console estimate column), but the finalized invoice contains token charges attributed to the tool outputs. Impact: LexiHealth reports a $9,200 unexpected charge on their monthly invoice tied to streamed sessions that used function calls for structured data retrieval. Environment: Production, us-east-1, enterprise account with reserved capacity and hosted fallback enabled. This affects streaming chat endpoints where the model returns tool invocations (function calls) and the tool outputs are streamed back as separate chunks.

Expected behavior: Console and estimate API should include a consistent token estimate for tool outputs (either zero if non-billable or the same token estimate used for invoice attribution). Final invoice should not introduce previously-unestimated token charges for tool outputs.

Actual behavior: Console estimate showed zero tool-output tokens for ~1.5M tokens across all tool invocations during the billing window. Final invoice shows ~1.45M billed tokens under a line labeled "tool-invoke-output" with reserved-rate fallback applied.

Customer impact: Revenue-impacting billing dispute, customer is holding renewal request pending resolution.

Attachments: Customer provided sample request IDs and screenshot of console estimate vs invoice lines.

title: Halogen Energy — fanout stall causing streaming silence, duplicate responses, and invoice exposure (post-incident follow-up)
preview: Issue summary: During a high-concurrency scheduled batch for Halogen Energy (enterprise, prod us-east), the request fanout path experienced a sustained stall resulting in partial streaming responses followed by duplicate completions. Impact: 11% of in-flight user sessions experienced either (a) a streaming silence lasting 6–18s before a replayed completion, or (b) duplicate completions delivered after client-side retry. Billing exposure: duplicate tokens were recorded for 0.8% of requests between 2026-03-10T09:42Z and 2026-03-10T10:26Z. User-visible effect: client-facing SDKs reported truncated transcripts and duplicated final messages; several Halogen automated workflows misinterpreted duplicates and triggered payment processing retrials. Environment: production, us-east, Dedicated pool: ded-pool-us-east-3, routing policy: capacity-first with multipath fanout to 3 backends. Time window: 2026-03-10T09:42Z — 2026-03-10T10:26Z. Steps to reproduce: 1) Replay customer traffic (provided capture) through multipath fanout; 2) Observe long tail in request-to-first-byte and duplicated completions when scheduler replays to alternate backend; 3) Reproduce with stress harness targeting redwood-xl-quantized at sequence lengths >800 tokens. Observed behavior: stalled mux in front of streaming worker pools (mux did not surface 5xx, but stopped forwarding chunks), scheduler retried to alternate node which completed the request again; dedup headers were inconsistently applied during the transient. Temporary mitigation performed: emergency rollback of a recent scheduler change (PR 427) and temporary reduction of fanout concurrency to 1 per request (via routing policy override).

title: session-kv-lease-sticky-causing-resumed-conversation-p99-latency-spike
preview:
Issue summary: ClearHealth reported a series of resumed conversational requests (user resumes session after short disconnect) that observe elevated tail latency (p99 ~4.2s) and occasional timeouts, compared to normal p99 ~250ms. This occurs after sessions have been inactive for ~30-90s. Impact: user-facing latency spikes and a small number of 504 timeouts causing degraded UX in production. Behavior is reproducible for certain session IDs and high-frequency resume patterns.

Context: This ticket was opened by Support after customer escalation. Initial triage indicates the serving runtime enters a blocking cold-path when a session key is considered "leased" by another backend shard due to a lease state that does not expire promptly. The behavior started after the kvcache lease change in release v2.18.

title: On-demand delta CSV export with UTC normalization, rotating MD5 manifest, and proof-of-exclusion for deleted records
preview:
Issue summary: Customer requests a one-off delta export of their tenant activity as a CSV with strict UTC timestamp normalization, a rotating MD5 manifest for each batch file, and a machine-verifiable 'proof-of-exclusion' artifact listing records deleted since the last snapshot. They need the package for a regulatory evidence submission and require deterministic ordering and replayability.

Requirements: (1) CSV delta with canonical UTC timestamps and ISO8601 formatting; (2) each batch file accompanied by an MD5 manifest entry and a signed manifest file (HMAC-SHA256 with their provided per-tenant key); (3) a separate "proof-of-exclusion" file: a compact list of deleted record IDs with per-ID deletion-tombstone hashes and a top-level Merkle-root signed by Redwood to prove non-inclusion in prior snapshots; (4) resume tokens and offsets for replay; (5) timezone normalization mapping notes and a field-translation glossary in the bundle.

Why unique: Customer explicitly needs evidence that certain records were omitted (deleted) and wants cryptographic proof to present to compliance that deletions are auditable and non-reversible in the delivered export.

title: Tool acknowledgement lost after client-side backpressure causing ambiguous replay and side-effect uncertainty
preview:
Issue summary: Customer reports that when their client applies aggressive client-side backpressure (pause/resume on the streaming socket) while the SDK/edge is in the middle of a function/tool call, the tool acknowledgement gets lost. Impact: downstream systems observe either duplicate side-effects after replay or never-observed confirmations, leading to billing ambiguity and customer data inconsistencies.

Environment: Prod, dedicated deployment for Nimbus Health in us-east. Python SDK 1.8.2 used from a serverless worker behind an in-house proxy.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Start long-lived streaming session via Python SDK to call external tool via function-calling feature.
2) Trigger a tool execution that emits a confirmation webhook synchronously from the tool handler.
3) Simulate client backpressure by pausing the TCP read loop for ~500-1500ms intermittently (server in CPU-constrained environment).
4) Resume the stream; SDK reconnects/flows may replay inflight events.

Expected behavior: SDK/edge should preserve tool acknowledgement idempotently and surface a single confirmed execution to the customer; replays should be deduplicated by idempotency keys.

Actual behavior: In ~30% of runs, the webhook confirmation is not recorded in the session state; later replay either re-invokes the tool (duplicate side-effect) or the system marks the execution as unknown.

Logs/observations:
- Edge logs show 'ack-window-timeout' correlated with paused reads at timestamp ranges.
- Kinesis traces show the outbound webhook was accepted (200 OK) but the confirmation event did not reach session persistence due to a write timeout to Redis during the pause window.
- Customer observed two charges for the same tool call in billing logs for one replayed invocation.

Customer impact: Potential duplicate side-effects and billing disputes. Requires mitigation guidance and a reliable long-term fix.

title: Reserved pool throughput drop from rapid tenant scatter causing slot thrash and GPU allocation shortfalls
preview: Issue summary: During the customer's recurring midday window we observed a sustained p95/p99 throughput degradation for NovaDocs' reserved pool that coincided with a large number of short-lived tenant connections spreading across many slots (tenant scatter). Impact: customer requests experience 3x p99 increase and burst 429/RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED for batched routes, causing downstream timeouts for user-facing documents ingestion pipelines. Environment: production reserved pool (dedicated) in us-east. Observed starting 2026-03-10 11:40 UTC. Steps to reproduce: 1) Replay customer traffic (small, high-concurrency short-streams) into reserved pool using synthetic tenant generator 2) Observe slot churn and repeated small allocations 3) Watch autoscaler not react quickly due to many ephemeral allocations and scheduler fragmentation Observed behavior: rapid slot attach/detach, high scheduler RPC latencies, GPU allocation failures, increased queue length and autoscaler hysteresis. Expected behavior: pool should absorb tenant short-stream spikes without sustained p99 throughput degradation; autoscaler should scale smoothly or fall back to higher-capacity routing. Logs/metrics: see linked dashboards. Key error signatures include repeated "attach-timeout" and "slot-evict" traces plus GPU attach failures with low free-HBM headroom. Customer impact: production ingestion jobs delayed and some documents dropped after retries. Temporary mitigation requested by customer: route small streams to fallback shared pool until resolved.

title: SSE -> WebSocket upgrade race leads to truncated final chunks and missing attachment URL
preview:
Issue summary:
Customers see a truncated final output and missing attachment URL when the client performs an SSE stream that upgrades to WebSocket mid-response. The last few tokens (often the attachment link or final punctuation) are dropped and the stream terminates without the terminal metadata envelope.

Impact:
- KiteNote (enterprise) reports 12% of long-running generation requests lost last-line attachments during heavy traffic windows.
- Affects production traffic in us-west; some artifacts seen in us-east during cross-region failover.

Environment:
prod - us-west, api gateway behind ALB -> edge proxy cluster -> serving-runtime pool. Customer using SDK-js v1.2.4 with automatic SSE-to-WS upgrade enabled.

Expected behavior:
When a client upgrades an open SSE stream to a WebSocket (preferred for bi-directional control), the server should deliver remaining buffered tokens and the final metadata envelope ("event: done" + attachments list) exactly once.

Actual behavior:
During an upgrade window the stream sometimes closes with a partial final chunk; the JSON/metadata envelope for attachments is missing or arrives but with empty attachments array. The socket on the client shows a 101 upgrade then a FIN shortly after with no more data.

Attachments/Logs referenced below.

title: Progress-callbacks + client-side retry causing unacknowledged tool executions and billing confusion
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer uses the TypeScript SDK in a serverless worker to issue long-running orchestrations that include multiple tool/function calls per user request. They've enabled progress callbacks (per-action progress events) so their frontend can display live status. Recently they observed duplicate billing for tool executions: the backend logs show the tool run completed, but the client timed out waiting for the final progress acknowledgement and retried the whole orchestration, which triggered the tooling to re-execute. The customer is requesting guidance on how to avoid unverified double-executions and how to reconcile charges.

Impact:
- Customer reports 2-8% increase in tooling cost for a specific heavy workflow.
- UI shows transient "stuck" state when progress callbacks are lost; sometimes user retries from browser which results in duplicate downstream effects (e.g., duplicate emails).
- Customer is concerned about side effects and billing.

Environment:
- TypeScript SDK (serverless): sdk-js v1.8.2
- Node.js 18 running in CloudRun-like environment behind a proxy with aggressive request timeouts (15s).
- Model: redwood/gpt-medium-1 pinned.
- Region: us-east.

Steps to reproduce (as reported by customer):
1) Start orchestration that fires three sequential tool calls with progress events enabled.
2) Simulate a proxy 15s timeout during the final streaming acknowledgement.
3) Client performs a retry of the orchestration (retry-on-timeout).
4) Server-side logs show first run succeeded and emitted tool completions, but the client retry triggers a second set of tool runs.

Expected behavior:
- Either the retry should detect previous successful run and not re-execute side-effectful tools (idempotent behavior), or progress/ack should be robustly delivered so client doesn't retry unnecessarily.

Observed logs (snippets provided by customer):
- \"[2026-03-08T11:05:12Z] tool_execute START toolId=pay-processor txn=tx-7a3f\"
- \"[2026-03-08T11:05:14Z] tool_execute COMPLETE toolId=pay-processor txn=tx-7a3f status=200\"
- \"[2026-03-08T11:05:27Z] client_timeout waiting for final ack, connection closed\"
- \"[2026-03-08T11:05:28Z] RETRY orchestration re-submitted idempotency_key=missing\"
- \"[2026-03-08T11:05:28Z] tool_execute START toolId=pay-processor txn=tx-9c5b\"

Additional context from customer:
- They rely on progress callbacks to display per-tool status in frontend and to allow manual retry of failed steps.
- They were not explicitly setting per-tool idempotency metadata in the orchestration payload because they assumed the SDK would propagate a top-level idempotency key to nested tool invocations.

Logs and attachments: customer attached a debug trace and a cURL reproduction script.

The customer is requesting guidance on how to avoid unverified double-executions and how to reconcile charges.

Impact:
- Customer reports 2-8% increase in tooling cost for a specific heavy workflow.
- UI shows transient "stuck" state when progress callbacks are lost; sometimes user retries from browser which results in duplicate downstream effects (e.g., duplicate emails).
- Customer is concerned about side effects and billing.

Environment:
- TypeScript SDK (serverless): sdk-js v1.8.2
- Node.js 18 running in CloudRun-like environment behind a proxy with aggressive request timeouts (15s).
- Model: redwood/gpt-medium-1 pinned.
- Region: us-east.

Steps to reproduce (as reported by customer):
1) Start orchestration that fires three sequential tool calls with progress events enabled.
2) Simulate a proxy 15s timeout during the final streaming acknowledgement.
3) Client performs a retry of the orchestration (retry-on-timeout).
4) Server-side logs show first run succeeded and emitted tool completions, but the client retry triggers a second set of tool runs.

Expected behavior:
- Either the retry should detect previous successful run and not re-execute side-effectful tools (idempotent behavior), or progress/ack should be robustly delivered so client doesn't retry unnecessarily.

Observed logs (snippets provided by customer):
- \"[2026-03-08T11:05:12Z] tool_execute START toolId=pay-processor txn=tx-7a3f\"
- \"[2026-03-08T11:05:14Z] tool_execute COMPLETE toolId=pay-processor txn=tx-7a3f status=200\"
- \"[2026-03-08T11:05:27Z] client_timeout waiting for final ack, connection closed\"
- \"[2026-03-08T11:05:28Z] RETRY orchestration re-submitted idempotency_key=missing\"
- \"[2026-03-08T11:05:28Z] tool_execute START toolId=pay-processor txn=tx-9c5b\"

Additional context from customer:
- They rely on progress callbacks to display per-tool status in frontend and to allow manual retry of failed steps.
- They were not explicitly setting per-tool idempotency metadata in the orchestration payload because they assumed the SDK would propagate a top-level idempotency key to nested tool invocations.

Logs and attachments: customer attached a debug trace and a cURL reproduction script.

title: Differential audit-merge manifest for PSP reconciliation and compliance evidence
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer (Orchid Bank, enterprise) requires a differential (delta) audit-merge manifest that maps Redwood activity records to their internal PSP transaction IDs for reconciliation and regulator evidence. They want cryptographically verifiable manifests (per-batch HMAC) plus a small sample payload demonstrating mapping logic for two days of high-volume traffic.

Impact:
- Orchid Bank cannot complete month-end PSP reconciliation without the mapping manifest and cryptographic proof. Potential regulatory reporting delay.
- High priority due to accounting/legal timelines for transaction dispute windows.

Environment:
- Production (us-east primary, eu-west failover).
- Customer uses Redwood hosted API and writes audit exports to their S3 bucket (s3://orchid-bank-redwood/exports/).

Requested deliverables from Redwood Support/Eng: 
1) A differential audit-merge manifest format spec (JSONL) that includes: export_window_start/end, per-record redwood_event_id, timestamp_utc, customer_psp_id, payment_reference, HMAC-SHA256 (per-batch) and batch_checksum (sha256).
2) One on-demand sample delta bundle for 2026-02-28 -> 2026-03-01 (max 5k records) with signed manifest and accompanying index.csv mapping redwood_event_id -> customer_psp_id.
3) Documentation: example ingestion snippet for their PSP system and a short verification script (python) to validate HMAC and checksums.
4) Statement of retention/ttl semantics and legal-hold override procedure (how to request snapshot pinning).

Steps to reproduce / what we will provide to Eng: 
- Customer S3 prefix: s3://orchid-bank-redwood/exports/delta/2026-02-28_to_2026-03-01/
- Sample query they run: SELECT * FROM audit_events WHERE event_time BETWEEN '2026-02-28T00:00:00Z' AND '2026-03-01T00:00:00Z' AND service = 'payments' LIMIT 5000;
- Expected mapping: event.metadata.transaction_id => customer_psp_id (customer provided mapping rules).

Constraints and questions we asked the customer: 
- Confirm whether the PSP ID should be written into the customer_psp_id field (they confirmed yes).
- Confirm acceptable signing key rotation window (customer requests daily-rotated KEK with published thumbprints).

Acceptance criteria:
- Manifest follows spec and is verifiable by their auditor script.
- Delivered sample bundle for the two-day window and proof-of-delivery to their secure SFTP endpoint or the S3 prefix above.

Notes on sensitivity: bundles contain payment transaction references and must be treated as highly sensitive (PII/financial). Delivery must follow Orchid Bank's secure delivery requirements (AES-256-GCM encrypted ZIP with pre-shared key + per-file signature).

title: IdP-initiated SLO probe truncates session cookie domain causing re-auth loops and 401s in private VPC
preview: Issue summary: After configuring IdP-initiated SSO for Nimbus Health's Private deployment, periodic IdP single-logout (SLO) probes from their vendor (AuthWave) are observed to cause the Redwood ingress to rewrite the session cookie domain by truncating tenant namespace suffixes. Impact: End-users and service accounts intermittently hit 401/reauth loops across the Redwood admin and ingest APIs. Expected: SLO probes should not alter or truncate session cookie domain/path; logout events should be processed without breaking other sessions. Environment details: Private control plane deployed into customer's VPC using edge-proxy v2.1.7, ingress-proxy v3.4.0, auth-bridge v1.9. Deployed certs: internal mTLS + customer-managed IdP metadata via SAML. Observed window: recurring since 2026-03-09 02:00 UTC, roughly every 6–8 hours when IdP health probes run.

title: Preflight safety check requests are being billed against tenant quota and causing 429s for interactive traffic
preview:
Issue summary: Customer runs a mixed workload where each interactive chat request is preceded by a lightweight 'preflight' safety/scoring check. After enabling a preflight microservice, FinSight started seeing persistent 429 throttles for low-latency interactive chat traffic. Impact: end-users get immediate 429s for short chat requests despite overall tenant usage being within expected bounds. The customer reports that each chat interaction produces two quota decrements in the console: one for preflight and one for the actual generation.

Expected behavior: preflight checks are lightweight and should either be exempt from tenant token accounting (internal preflight) or billed at a small fixed cost, not double-counting the real generation request. Observed behavior: preflight + generation both decrement tenant token buckets as full requests, leading to tenant quota exhaustion and 429s that affect production chat traffic.

title: High attach latency and throughput collapse from small-chunk inference stream CQ saturation
preview:
Issue summary: BrightWrite (dedicated pool) reported a mid-morning collapse in steady-state throughput for multiple batched routes. Impact: p50/p95 token throughput drop ~40-70% for routed models and p99 attach latency spiking to 1.2s from baseline ~80ms. Customer-facing inference requests saw elevated 5xx attach timeouts and increased fallbacks to smaller model variants. Environment: prod us-west dedicated pool, heterogeneous GPU hosts (A100 80GB + A10 mixed), customer traffic characterized by many short inference streams (~12-32 tokens) at high qps.

We observed a pattern inconsistent with normal autoscaler signals: autoscaler did not trigger additional hosts despite attach latency rising. Support triaged as potential noisy-tenant + kernel-level IO backpressure (PCIe completion queue saturation) affecting attach path and the serving scheduler fairness.

Impact details: Customer reported 30% drop in successful completions during business-hour window, increased errors correlated with sudden bursts of thousands of small requests per second.

Runbook links above for quick reference.

title: Long-tail prompt length burst causes KV-cache fragmentation, runtime OOMs and elevated 5xx (apigw 503)
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer CurrentsAI reported a sudden spike of 503/5xx responses starting 2026-03-10 02:10 UTC for a subset of their requests. Error rate peaked ~18% for the tenant for ~45 minutes.

Impact:
- Tenant-facing latency increased and many streaming sessions were terminated with 503.
- Several serving-runtime pods experienced OOM kills and restarted; autoscaler reacted by adding nodes which increased churn.
- Customer reports multiple failed inference calls and degraded UX for production users.

Timeline (high-level):
- 02:08 UTC: tenant traffic shows a shift: 90th/99th prompt length jumped from ~400 tokens to ~6k tokens (long-tail burst from batch import job).
- 02:10 UTC: API Gateway backlog increases, 503s observed and routed to support alerts.
- 02:15 - 02:40 UTC: multiple runtime pods report GPU memory fragmentation and OOM; KV cache evictions spike.
- 02:45 UTC: mitigations applied (per-tenant throttling) and customer backoff; error rates return to baseline by 03:00 UTC.

Environment and context:
- Dedicated tenant on us-west-2, pinned capacity to a dedicated GPU pool.
- Autoscaler configured with reactive scale-up; no hard per-tenant concurrency limit before incident.
- Runtime version: 2026.02.14-release (includes prefetching + KV cache compaction).

Steps to reproduce (approx):
1) Replay tenant \"large import\" job that issues many long prompts (median 4k tokens, tail to 12k).
2) Allow concurrent submission at existing customer concurrency (50 concurrent streaming sessions).
3) Observe serving-runtime logs and apigw backlog.

Observed behavior / logs (selected snippets):
- apigw: \"upstream response status 503 -- upstream reset while waiting for connection\"
- runtime stdout: \"OOM on GPU 0: failed to allocate 284 MB; fragmentation detected\"
- kvcache metrics: eviction_rate spike from 0.2/s to 38/s; cache_hit_ratio dropped 83% -> 27%
- autoscaler: scaling_event: added 3 nodes in 2 minutes; pod_restarts increased by 4

Customer impact note:
Customer reported failed imports and UI errors for paying prod users; business impact moderate-high. Customer requested root cause and mitigation; asked for rollback if necessary.

Immediate actions taken by Support (timeline):
- 02:22 UTC: Applied temporary tenant-level concurrency cap at gateway (20 concurrent) and limited max_tokens to 4096 for new streaming sessions.
- 02:30 UTC: Opened internal incident channel (#support, #sre, #runtime).
- 02:40 UTC: Requested emergency patch to prefetch settings on the dedicated pool (reduce prefetch window).

Additional notes:
- This looks different from prior kv-cache thrash incidents: root cause appears to be extreme prompt-length skew rather than constant high qps.
- Prefetch combined with long-tail prompts appears to oversubscribe GPU memory and cause fragmentation rather than a simple allocation spike.

preview:
Issue summary:
Customer LexiData reports steady stream of 429 responses for an otherwise healthy multipart embedding import that started after they switched to a pooled-proxy + short-lived signed URL callback flow. The errors are concentrated during concurrent callback fanout and appear as sustained short-window throttles (soft 429s) rather than a single burst.

Impact:
- Production embedding backfill stalled for multiple jobs, causing downstream search freshness degradation for LexiData customers.
- Errors began ~2026-03-09 18:10 UTC and persist intermittently across the affected import windows.

User-reported context:
- They moved from direct single-host uploads to a pooled proxy (customer-side) that multiplexes callbacks to our signed-url endpoint.
- Each signed URL rotation results in ~50 short-lived callbacks at once.
- Happens only in us-east region and only for multipart embedding imports, normal single-record embedding calls unaffected.

title: SSE stream truncates and resumes with interleaved metadata after edge cache chunk insertion
preview:
Issue summary:
Customers observe SSE streams terminating early (HTTP 200 closed) with a partial JSON payload, then receiving a resumed stream that injects an edge-cache metadata chunk in the middle of the response. The resumed payload has metadata frames interleaved with tokens, which client SDKs mis-interpret and assemble into malformed JSON.

Impact:
- Multiple API consumers at Foodly see truncated recommendations or garbled JSON in ~2-3% of long streaming responses (conversations > 4k tokens).
- Causing client-side JSON.parse failures and repeated retries. Some retries lead to duplicated prefixes.

Environment:
- Dedicated customer tenant on prod-us-east using dedicated routing through our edge CDN (FastEdge).
- Streaming via SSE, client uses our JS SDK v2.3.1 behind a corporate proxy.

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1) Client issues long-stream generation request (max_tokens ~ 4096) using redwood-instruct-7b with streaming SSE.
2) At ~20s into the stream (token boundary varies), edge proxy logs show an asynchronous cache insert for a previous short request on the same connection.
3) Connection is gracefully closed by edge (HTTP/1.1 finish) with 200. SSE client receives partial JSON body up to the last complete choice.
4) Client reconnects with resume token; server responds with remaining chunks but an edge-side cache-insert control chunk (X-Edge-Cache-Insert: true) is appended between token frames.
5) SDK concatenation yields JSON with stray metadata lines, client parse fails.

Observed behavior:
- Partial payloads and then resumed payloads containing unexpected cache-metadata frames (text lines like "EDGE-CACHE-INSERT: id=...;len=128;src=fastedge").
- Client SDK treats these as part of the stream payload; final JSON is invalid.
- Server/tracing shows worker did not re-encode metadata as SSE comment lines ("comment: ...") and the edge inserted raw lines when performing insert-on-the-fly.

Expected behavior:
- Edge should not insert or mutate in-stream content for active SSE sessions. Any edge/insert control must be encoded as SSE comments (starting with ":") or handled out-of-band so client SDKs ignore them.

Logs / snippets:
- Edge traces (fastedge): "cache-insert: attach_chunk id=abc123 connection=conn-77 offset=98123"
- API gateway: "200 finish connection conn-77 cause=cache_insert_flush"
- Serving runtime: "session-resume request_id=rid-9f2 remaining_bytes=2345"
- Client error: "SyntaxError: Unexpected token 'E' at position 12"

Investigation notes:
- Reproduced on our internal harness when we enabled FastEdge caching in front of a dedicated routing pool.
- The edge process performed a synchronous cache insert that wrote an ASCII metadata line without SSE comment prefix, because it re-used an HTTP body buffer path intended for non-streamed responses.
- When client reconnects with resume token, the serving-runtime merges remaining KV cache tokens and appends the buffered edge payload, resulting in interleaved metadata.
- Not observed when traffic bypasses FastEdge or when edge caching is turned off.
- Affected models correlate with longer output sessions; short responses unaffected.

Workaround:
- For immediate mitigation, advise Foodly to disable FastEdge insert-on-write for their dedicated route (edge flag: cache_insert=false) or reduce max_tokens to keep streams short (<1.5k tokens).
- Temporary server-side mitigation: guard in serving-runtime to drop lines matching /^EDGE-.*INSERT:/ on resume merge (hotfix candidate).

Next steps / proposed fix:
- SRE to add an edge change: ensure cache inserts use SSE-safe comment prefix or are buffered out-of-band (edge ticket in progress).
- Runtime: add strict sanitizer on resumed streams to strip non-SSE comment lines and validate JSON boundaries before emitting to client.
- QA: create regression tests in streaming harness that simulate cache-insert events under load.

Please see comments below for back-and-forth and attach request/response dumps if needed.

title: Embedding padding/trim mismatch causing rerank order oscillation and autoscaling surge
preview: Issue summary: AtlasDocs reports intermittent rerank quality regressions (top-k order flips) coincident with brief 500/timeout spikes and a burst of autoscaling activity. Impact: customer search relevance fluctuates for ~5-30s windows, end-users see wrong top results and increased latency. Observed primarily on dedicated pool in us-east. title: Tenant webhook audit stream: mutual-TLS client cert rotation, backfill requests, and SOC2 evidence bundle
preview:
Issue summary:
GreenSpring Health uses our tenant-scoped webhook endpoint for real-time audit delivery. During a scheduled client certificate rotation on 2026-03-09 they reported a gap: ~18k audit events (approx. 3 hours) did not arrive at their collector. They need (1) a validated backfill of the missing events for tenant T-GREEN-12 with chronological ordering preserved, (2) cryptographic proof of the server-side signing/checkpoint used during the backfill window, and (3) a signed artifact demonstrating the client cert rotation timeline (previous cert thumbprint, rotation timestamp, and validation that no events were re-delivered outside the window).

Impact:
- Missing audit records for regulated ePHI ingestion pipeline (GreenSpring Health, HIPAA scope).
- Customer cannot complete urgent SOC2 evidence packet for their auditor until audit continuity is proven.

Environment:
- Tenant: T-GREEN-12
- Delivery method: webhook (POST to customer collector) with mutual TLS (client cert).
- Redwood endpoint: https://audit-delivery.prod-us-west.redwood.ai/tenant/T-GREEN-12/events
- Delivery window affected: 2026-03-09 02:10:00 -> 05:15:00 UTC

Steps to reproduce (observed by customer):
1) Customer rotated client certs at their collector at 2026-03-09 02:00 UTC.
2) Between 02:10--05:15 UTC they observed TLS handshake failures and 502/504 responses in their collector logs.
3) They validated our webhook retries but note their collector returned early 421/ENCRYPT errors for the rotated cert for ~3 hours.
4) Post-rotation (after successful TLS handshake) delivery resumed but missing chronological records were not backfilled.

Relevant logs and sample curl for reproduction (customer provided):
- Collector error snippet: "tls: client certificate signed by unknown authority" followed by "http: proxy error: upstream response timeout"
- Sample curl they used to test mutual TLS: curl --cert client.pem --key client.key -X POST https://audit-delivery.prod-us-west.redwood.ai/tenant/T-GREEN-12/events -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{\"event\":\"test\"}'

Request from customer:
- Backfill the missing events for the specified window in an append-only, time-ordered payload.
- Produce a signed manifest for the backfill (SHA256-based manifest and server signing key/thumbprint used).
- Provide a short timeline that shows server-side delivery attempts and TLS handshake statuses mapped to UTC timestamps and request IDs.
- Supply the cryptographic evidence of the client-cert rotation acceptance (server logs showing previous cert thumbprint, rotation acceptance timestamp, and per-request CN/subject mapping where available).

Attachments: customer-provided collector logs (attached), example curl invocation, and SOC2 evidence checklist from their auditor.

title: Unexpected 5xx spike driven by tenant throttle, accept-queue growth and runtime worker flood
preview:
Issue summary:
Customers observed a sharp rise in 5xx responses across multiple endpoints for a subset of tenants in us-east-1 starting ~2026-03-10T20:05Z. The pattern included high API Gateway accept-queue depth, connection keepalive churn, and repeated runtime worker restarts leading to upstream 502/503 errors.

Impact:
- Lumina Analytics (dedicated) reported increased API failures affecting interactive chat and short-streaming sessions (~30% failure rate for a 5-minute window).
- Broader, low-level 5xx floor visible for ~12 tenants sharing the same routing shard.

Environment:
- prod us-east-1
- Affected routing shard: shard-e-12
- Serving runtime version: 2026.02.11-hotfix

Observed timeline (UTC):
- 2026-03-10T20:03: service-level alarms start triggering on apigw accept-queue (>1500).
- 2026-03-10T20:05: Lumina support case created; we see elevated 5xx percentages at api layer.
- 2026-03-10T20:07: Autoscaler autoscaled runtime pool; new ephemeral workers spawned rapidly.
- 2026-03-10T20:09: Multiple runtime worker processes OOM or exit with GLIBC symbol errors; downstream 502s spike.
- 2026-03-10T20:18: Support team applied temporary priority-shed for noisy tenant and drained several ephemeral workers; error rate fell to baseline by ~20:30.

Steps to reproduce (observed pattern, not deterministic):

title: Console widget load latency / 504s when trace-hook request headers exceed 16KB
preview:
Issue summary: Several LuminaHealth users report Dashboard widgets failing to render or timing out (504) intermittently when the underlying traces include very large trace-hook header payloads (base64-encoded span attributes).

Impact: Affects multiple dashboard panels that include trace links. Charts either hang in 'loading' state for 15-30s then 504, or render partial data with missing trace links. This prevents on-call and SRE from drilling into traces during incidents and has impacted two production incident investigations in the last 48h.

Environment: Production, us-east-1. Issue started after deployment of trace-hook header expansion change (round-robin agent enrichment) rolled on 2026-03-09. Customer on enterprise plan with Route policy pinned to us-east ingestion.

Observed behavior: When trace metadata headers attached to trace-hook callbacks exceed ~16KB, the console widget requests to /api/console/panel/data show drastically increased latency or 504 errors. Requests with smaller header sizes succeed.

Expected behavior: Console should accept and process trace-hook header values up to the documented limit (32KB) without causing widget-level timeouts or partial renders; large header values may be trimmed by downstream services but should not cause 504s.

Notes: This looks like a regression linked to header forwarding changes in api-gateway/proxy-layer where oversized headers trigger buffering fallback and timeouts in proxy-to-console streaming path.

title: On-demand ordered entitlement-change ledger slice with encrypted manifest and HMAC batch proofs for legal review
preview:
Issue summary: Finverity Bank (enterprise) requests a forensically-ordered slice of entitlement-change events for a two-week window (2026-02-22 to 2026-03-07) for legal review. They require per-batch HMAC proofs, an encrypted manifest that lists sequence ranges and checksums, and delivery to their SFTP endpoint with PGP encryption.

Impact: Customer legal team cannot complete subpoena review until they receive verifiable, ordered audit artifacts. This is blocking a regulatory deadline.

Request details: Provide a chronological (event-time ordered) ledger export that includes: subject identifier (pseudonymized per prior agreement), change type (grant/revoke/modify), actor principal, timestamp (UTC), request_id/trace_id, and pre-/post- entitlement state where available. Deliverables must include: 1) NDJSON export slices (max 100k events per file), 2) per-file SHA256 checksum, 3) manifest.json.gpg encrypted with customer's public key listing filename -> sequence_range -> checksum -> HMAC (batch-level), and 4) HMAC verification keys signed by Redwood's KMS attestation (certificate chain).

Security constraints: Do not include raw PII beyond pseudonymized subject ids; adhere to the customer's approved redaction map (reference attached). Use AES-256 server-side encryption for intermediate storage. Signed manifest must include KEK rotation metadata and the KID used to sign HMACs.

Delivery preference: SFTP to sftp.finverity.example:2222 in passive mode, PGP-encrypted payloads. If SFTP fails, fallback to pre-signed S3 objects with time-limited access (24h).

Contact: Finverity legal contact - legal.delivery@finverity.example (vouch available), Redwood customer success - Priya Nair.

Support notes / acceptance criteria: Files must be ordered by event_time (not ingestion_time). Manifest must validate with provided KID. Provide a short verification script (bash/Python) for the customer to hash-verify files and validate HMAC chain.

title: Edge NAT handshake interruption results in incomplete numbered checklist + missing embedded image on SSE stream
preview:
Issue summary: Customer reports that long SSE streaming responses intermittently terminate with the last numbered checklist item missing and a final embedded image URL omitted. This happens after their users experience a network path change (ISP NAT port refresh / IP rebind).

Impact: Several LuminaDocs customers see corrupted docs: a numbered checklist in the final paragraphs is cut at item 3 of 4 and the final image embed (markdown ![diagram](...)) never arrives. Problem first reported 2026-03-10; frequency ~2-3% of long (>= 2k token) streams for their users.

Environment: production (us-east), customer on dedicated plan with VPC egress through their ISP (residential & corporate). Affected model: gpt-open-xt-4b.

Steps to reproduce (customer):
1) Start SSE request that streams a long markdown document containing a 4-item numbered checklist and an inline image URL near the end.
2) While stream active, client environment briefly changes IP (customer observed ISP NAT port refresh during mobile tethering).
3) Client reconnects automatically. SSE resumes, but stream ends with checklist missing last bullet and no image URL.

Observed behavior: On reconnect the SSE stream completes without an explicit end marker in the original session; the client sees the final chunk cut off between bullets (partial sentence) and image markdown absent. Server logs show the upstream worker closed the old socket and a new socket established with a different connection-id; resume handshake attempted but resume_offset reported by client does not match server session snapshot (resume rejected).

Expected behavior: Stream resume should deliver the remaining tokens (or clearly return an error) so the client can render the complete checklist and image; resume should be tolerant to customer NAT port refresh where only 5-tuple changes but session identity remains logically the same via resume token.

Logs / snippets: (sanitized):
- edge-proxy: connection closed, reason=half-close, remote_ip=198.51.100.23:52345, conn_id=wrk-1172
- serving-runtime: resume-token=abc123 ttl_expiry=2026-03-10T12:03:05Z, compare_offset=15670, client_offset=15670, session_state=sealed
- worker: flush_tail: aborted before final event, kv_cache_seal=true, client_resume_reject: mismatch-epoch

Customer-supplied curl sequence (simplified):
- initial SSE: GET /v1/streams?resume_token=abc123 -> stream begins
- simulate NAT rebind (tether off/on)
- new SSE connect with same resume_token -> server responded 200 but returned truncated body

title: Scheduled dashboard snapshot suppressed when query includes tracehook + aliased route identifiers
preview:
Issue summary:
Scheduled snapshot jobs for dashboards that include a query-level tracehook (tracehook param in the query) are being suppressed and not persisted when the query references aliased route identifiers (route aliases used by the customer).

Impact:
- OrbitPay scheduled daily snapshots for 20 dashboards show 'Last run: succeeded' but no snapshot file appears in the scheduled_reports listing and email reports are empty for affected dashboards.
- This affects reporting and SLO visibility for two top-level product teams during their monthly review.
- Customer-visible data loss for scheduled exports (not ad-hoc snapshots).

Environment:
- Prod (us-east-1)
- Tenant: orbitpay-prod (enterprise)
- Dashboards affected are high-cardinality, use route aliasing for multi-tenant routes and include tracehook parameters in WHERE clauses.

Steps to reproduce (minimal):
1) Create dashboard with a saved query that includes a tracehook parameter: WHERE tracehook = 'org=orbitpay' AND route_alias = 'billing-tenant::v2/pay'
2) Configure a scheduled snapshot (daily) for that dashboard.
3) Wait for scheduled runner to execute or trigger the scheduler job manually via Console > Scheduled snapshots > Run now.
4) Observe that job completes with exit code 0 (runner logs), Console shows 'Last run: succeeded' but no snapshot file, and notification emails are empty.

Observed behavior:
- Scheduled job logs show success and a short warning about 'route id normalization', but no error.
- Snapshot manifest generation step is skipped silently for queries that matched alias-normalized route values.
- No entry is written to the scheduled_reports object-store keyspace.

Expected behavior:
- Snapshot job produces a snapshot artifact and writes a manifest to scheduled_reports and triggers notification emails when configured.

Temporary workaround:
- Edit the saved query to replace aliased route identifiers with their canonical route ids (customer confirmed this restores snapshots).
- Alternatively, remove tracehook from the saved query and use dashboard-level filtering (less ideal).

Notes:
- Customer confirmed this started after the 2026-03-03 deployment that included route alias normalization in the query-optimizer module and a patch to preserve tracehook tokens across scheduled-run serialization.
- This looks like an interaction between the alias normalization and the scheduled snapshot deduplication logic.

title: Promotional waiver applied to next billing cycle instead of current after dedicated fallback
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer BrightLearn Inc. reports a promotional waiver (30% overage credit, promo code: SPRINGENTERPRISE-30) intended to offset a disputed overage on the invoice for the current billing cycle (INV-2026-03-BLE-7301). Instead, the waiver line appeared as a provisional credit scheduled for the next invoice period, resulting in a short-term overcharge and an autopay failure for the current cycle.

Impact:
- Immediate financial impact: customer account temporarily charged $12,345.67 more than expected; autopay attempted and failed, triggering billing alerts.
- Business impact: customer procurement flagged the charge and paused integrations until billing is corrected.
- Affects BrightLearn production inference workloads in us-east and eu-west.

Environment:
- Tier: enterprise (dedicated GPU pool)
- Regions: us-east primary, eu-west fallback
- Components implicated: billing postback pipeline, dedicated-fallback routing logic, invoice finalization job

Steps to reproduce (observed by customer):
1. BrightLearn performed a capacity-heavy training-inference burst on 2026-03-02 that exceeded reserved buffer on us-east.
2. Traffic routed to eu-west dedicated fallback for a 1.5-hour window (03:12–04:42 UTC).
3. BrightLearn submitted billing support request for promo waiver (SPRINGENTERPRISE-30) on 2026-03-04 referencing the current invoice.
4. On invoice finalization (2026-03-05), billing shows the overage charge applied; the promo waiver did not offset the current invoice but shows a provisional credit pending for the next billing cycle.

Observed behavior:
- Waiver appears in billing ledger as a provisional credit with effective_period starting 2026-04-01.
- Postback logs show waiver acceptance event processed at 2026-03-04T18:23:47Z but linked to a future period idempotency key.
- Autopay attempted on 2026-03-06 and failed due to mismatch between expected balance and posted charges.

Expected behavior:
- Waiver requested and accepted before invoice finalization should be applied to the current open invoice and reduce the total due immediately.

Attachments / Logs:
- Customer provided invoice PDF (INV-2026-03-BLE-7301.pdf) and billing support request email thread.
- Extracts from postback-processing logs attached to the ticket (postback-2026-03-04.log).
text:
Issue summary:
Customer BrightLearn Inc. reports a promotional waiver (30% overage credit, promo code: SPRINGENTERPRISE-30) intended to offset a disputed overage on the invoice for the current billing cycle (INV-2026-03-BLE-7301). Instead, the waiver line appeared as a provisional credit scheduled for the next invoice period, resulting in a short-term overcharge and an autopay failure for the current cycle.

Impact:
- Immediate financial impact: customer account temporarily charged $12,345.67 more than expected; autopay attempted and failed, triggering billing alerts.
- Business impact: customer procurement flagged the charge and paused integrations until billing is corrected.
- Affects BrightLearn production inference workloads in us-east and eu-west.

Environment:
- Tier: enterprise (dedicated GPU pool)
- Regions: us-east primary, eu-west fallback
- Components implicated: billing postback pipeline, dedicated-fallback routing logic, invoice finalization job

Steps to reproduce (observed by customer):
1. BrightLearn performed a capacity-heavy training-inference burst on 2026-03-02 that exceeded reserved buffer on us-east.
2. Traffic routed to eu-west dedicated fallback for a 1.5-hour window (03:12–04:42 UTC).
3. BrightLearn submitted billing support request for promo waiver (SPRINGENTERPRISE-30) on 2026-03-04 referencing the current invoice.
4. On invoice finalization (2026-03-05), billing shows the overage charge applied; the promo waiver did not offset the current invoice but shows a provisional credit pending for the next billing cycle.

Observed behavior:
- Waiver appears in billing ledger as a provisional credit with effective_period starting 2026-04-01.
- Postback logs show waiver acceptance event processed at 2026-03-04T18:23:47Z but linked to a future period idempotency key.
- Autopay attempted on 2026-03-06 and failed due to mismatch between expected balance and posted charges.

Expected behavior:
- Waiver requested and accepted before invoice finalization should be applied to the current open invoice and reduce the total due immediately.

Attachments / Logs:
- Customer provided invoice PDF (INV-2026-03-BLE-7301.pdf) and billing support request email thread.
- Extracts from postback-processing logs attached to the ticket (postback-2026-03-04.log).

title: Sawtooth throttling pattern during customer daily batch sweep causing intermittent 429s
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer ScoutMetrics runs a nightly daily-sweep job that uploads ~10k small embedding requests in bursts every 7 minutes between 02:00-04:00 UTC. Over the last three nights they saw a repeating sawtooth pattern of 429 responses — throughput spikes then a periodic waveform of throttles for ~30s, recovers briefly, then spikes again. This is affecting their pipelines (15-25% of batches failed), causing downstream retries and significant time-to-completion regression.

Impact:
- Approx 15-25% of batch embedding requests during sweep windows return 429s.
- Customer reports downstream job run time increase from ~12m to ~45m.
- Visible errors in their job logs; customer unable to complete daily indexing reliably.

Environment:
- Tenant: ScoutMetrics (dedicated pool, committed capacity).
- Region: prod-us-east (pool id: pool-ussc-07 provided by customer).
- API path: POST /v1/embeddings (batch upload via multipart clients).
- Keys: rotating short-lived keys used by their workers.

Observed behavior / logs provided by customer:
- 2026-03-09 02:07:13 UTC - burst of 10k requests, API returns 80% 200, 20% 429 distributed in ~30s window.
- Graph provided by customer: sawtooth pattern of error-rate with ~7m periodicity (attached as customer_graph_20260309.png).
- Customer log snippets: "ERROR: 429 rate limit - token_bucket_exhausted for tenant=ScoutMetrics pool=pool-ussc-07"

Steps to reproduce (customer job):
1) Start 200 concurrent worker processes each uploading 50 small documents in parallel to /v1/embeddings.
2) Repeat this at 7 minute cadence for a 2-hour window.
3) Observe intermittent 429s clustered as waveform spikes rather than uniform throttling.

Request from customer:
- Confirm why sawtooth throttle waveform occurs despite dedicated pool commitment.
- Provide recommended config changes (burst window, token refill, multiplexing guidance) and short-term mitigations to avoid daily job failures.
- Request SRE help to review pool telemetry (token-bucket accounting, health-check increments) and verify no cross-tenant bleed or health-checks causing miscounting.

title: SSE: lingered TCP socket results in interleaved token fragments and missing final event on client reconnect
preview:
Issue summary: Customer reports that after a short mobile network switch the SSE stream resumes but the final 'end' event is missing and some token fragments are interleaved/duplicated. The behaviour is repeatable from the customer's environment when they trigger a cellular->wifi handoff. Impact: Produces incomplete outputs to end users and causes client-side reassembly failures; NoteFlow reported user-visible truncation in 3 of 10 resumed sessions and data loss for the last paragraph in many cases.

Observed behaviour: When client reconnects following a transient socket drop, the resumed stream sometimes contains duplicated prefix tokens, interleaved heartbeat frames, and the terminal 'event: done' or final JSON marker is not received. Client SDK shows partial last-choice and then the connection closes. Server traces show the backend had emitted the final marker.

Expected behaviour: Resume should stitch prior partials and deliver the final marker exactly once. No duplication of in-flight fragments and no suppression of end-event.

Context/notes: Customer uses Redwood Dedicated with reserved pool in us-east. They use our Node.js client SDK on mobile webview that falls back to SSE. Traffic flows through customer's corp Envoy + our api-gateway + internal edge Envoy. They have occasional short-lived path migrations (TCP 4-tuple change).

title: Conservative opt-tuning safety net: tenant sees intermittent 5xx and elevated tail latency after enabling kv-prefetch + q4 quant profile
preview:
Issue summary: After enabling a tenant-level optimizer profile that enables aggressive KV prefetch (window=32) and a 4-bit quant profile (q4_mixed) the customer reports intermittent 5xx errors and a noticeable P99 tail latency increase for interactive chat routes.

Impact: Intermittent 5xx responses (approx 0.6% of requests during peak) and P95->P99 jump from ~450ms to ~1.2s for the tenant's primary chat route. Customer reports visible user complaints and warned they may revert to a competitor if unresolved.

Environment: Dedicated pool in prod-us-east serving BrightPost Health tenant. Traffic is primarily short-turn chat (avg seq len 80 tokens). The change was applied during a low-traffic maintenance window two days ago and rolled to 100% tenant traffic 90 minutes after initial canary.

Observed behavior: 1) Spikes of 502/503 with upstream serving-runtime logs showing 'KV_CACHE_MISS' thrashing followed by 'INFERENCE_TIMEOUT' entries. 2) Node-level GPU memory pressure observed on 2/6 nodes in the tenant pool with ephemeral OOM logs during the same window. 3) Error rate correlates with a rise in 'async_batch_queue_time' metric and increased packet-drop on egress links.

Expected behavior: Stable error rate (<=0.01%) and tail latencies within baseline SLO (P95 < 500ms, P99 < 800ms).

Attachments: telemetry snippets, trace ids, and sample request/response pairs included in logs field below.

title: Midflight proxy upgrade causes websocket handoff to drop terminal JSON object and leave truncated choice
preview:
Issue summary: During a high-throughput window on 2026-03-10, AtlasApps reported that long-running WebSocket streams occasionally end with a partial JSON 'choice' object and no terminal metadata (end_event). Client logs show the final JSON object missing closing braces and the 'metadata' field absent. Impact: Several production chat flows returned truncated final sentences, sometimes causing downstream parsers to throw and the customer to re-request completions, doubling token usage and affecting SLAs.

Environment: Production (eu-west), requests routed through our edge proxy pool behind a CDN with active health checks and transparent connection rebinds. A small subset of requests go through an older proxy flavor that performs an in-flight socket upgrade to another edge on backend health probe.

Observed behavior: Client receives partial JSON chunk (last text tokens present, no "end" event). WS connection is closed by the client after a short timeout; server logs show an abrupt EOF on the upstream socket around the same timestamp. No end-event was recorded in the request trace.

Expected: On connection handoff or graceful close, the serving runtime should emit a terminal marker and all associated metadata prior to any socket teardown or proxy handoff so clients receive a complete final chunk.

Temporary impact mitigation requested by customer: recommend sticky sessions or disabling the specific edge health-rebind for the customer route while we investigate.

title: Sustained 429s during sliced ingest with priority class mismatch after dedicated pool migration
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports sustained 429 responses across a set of sliced ingest workers after migrating ingestion to a Dedicated pool. The traffic mix is: low-latency chat calls (priority=high) and large multi-part embedding uploads (priority=background). After the migration, background uploads are intermittently exhausting burst credits at the per-route layer and causing opaque 429s for high-priority short calls during peak windows.

Impact:
- ~30% of short chat requests returned 429 between 09:00-11:30 PDT on 2026-03-10 for Streamly AI.
- Customer reports user-facing errors and retry amplification in their orchestrator.
- Business impact: partial degradation of their real-time features during peak ingest windows.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool: dp-132-usw
- Region: us-west
- Ingest coordinator version: 2026.02.3
- Rate-limiter service version: 1.14.0-canary

Observed behaviour:
- Persistent 429s reported by customer for short-lived chat requests coincident with high concurrency uploads.
- Throttle headers (when present) show per-route-window exhaustion; some requests show no X-RateLimit headers (opaque 429).

Expected behaviour:
- High-priority interactive calls should be isolated into a small burst pool and not experience 429s while background uploads borrow burst credit from a separate pool (or be rate-limited gracefully).

Customer asks:
- Why are small chat calls getting 429s when their load is relatively small?
- How to configure priority classes and burst pools for dedicated pools to prevent this?
