title: API throttler feedback loop causing surge in 5xx errors and streaming session drops for PaperTrail Labs
preview: Issue summary: Starting 2026-03-10 03:40 UTC we observed a sustained elevation in 5xx responses from the API gateway for traffic belonging to PaperTrail Labs' dedicated tenant. Errors manifested as HTTP 502/503 from the gateway and abrupt streaming session terminations. Impact: Customer-facing failures for PaperTrail's realtime chat product (multiple streams dropped, reconnect storms). Scale: Affected roughly 6% of the tenant's requests during the spike window (~10:30-11:15 PDT), with short bursts peaking at ~18% error rate. Business impact: user-facing errors and automated client retries resulting in support escalations. Timeline: - 2026-03-10 03:37 UTC: Monitoring alert triggers (gateway 5xx rate increased). - 2026-03-10 03:40 UTC: Support opened ticket; customer reports streaming disconnects. - 2026-03-10 04:05 UTC: On-call SRE and ingress engineer pulled into Slack (#support and #incidents). - 2026-03-10 04:20 UTC: Initial mitigation applied (per-route rate cap lowered, soft-evict enabled). - 2026-03-10 05:00 UTC: Errors reduced but intermittent reconnect spikes continued. Observed behavior: - Connection drops reported by clients (RST/ERR stream close). - Gateway logs show short-lived upstream 502/503 with runtime returning 'upstream closed' and 'watchdog timeout' traces. - Traffic pattern: mix of short streaming sessions and frequent small inference requests; many clients retried within short backoffs. Steps to reproduce: see steps_to_reproduce field. Immediate mitigation: temporarily reverted a recent per-tenant throttler tuning deployed 2026-03-09 and increased keepalive pool size for the tenant's route. Result: immediate drop in 5xx rate to baseline, but long-tail reconnect churn persisted. Root cause hypothesis: A feedback loop between the gateway per-route token-bucket throttler and the serving-runtime backoff behavior. When the throttler began rejecting bursts, clients retried aggressively; those retries congested the ingress priority-shedder and caused rapid ephemeral connections that pushed runtimes into kernel-level timeouts and watchdog restarts under memory pressure. Secondary contributors: - Slightly aggressive throttler hysteresis (reduction window too large) introduced oscillation. - Autoscaler thresholds for the dedicated pool were tuned high for CPU (not socket backlog), so scale-up lagged. - A recent runtime patch changed reconnection jitter behavior, reducing effective jitter and concentrating retries. Current status: Mitigations in place; monitoring and customer observing. Next steps: - Engineering to introduce jittered exponential backoff in runtime client retry logic (PR created). - Tune token-bucket parameters to shorter window and lower reject penalty. - Adjust autoscaler to consider accept-queue length and open socket count. - Post-incident follow-up: write a runbook entry and bake tests for throttler hysteresis. Escalation: On-call SRE (Diego) and Platform Eng (Lina Park) engaged.

title: Streaming chat stalls mid-generation when Dedicated GPU worker triggers kernel memory compaction
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer BrightForms reports that SSE-based chat streams stop producing tokens mid-response (~after 200-600 tokens) but the HTTP connection remains open. The client sees no final event and ultimately times out after ~30s of silence. This is observed only on their Dedicated pool in us-west-2 and primarily with redwood-neo-13B-quant; some occurrences with redwood-14B-v1 were reported after model fallback.

Impact:
- Multiple end users see partial/paused responses in production web chat widgets.
- Reported across three customer sessions in the last 24 hours; affects important SLAs for BrightForms (enterprise contract).
- Workaround (client reconnect) recovers response but may produce duplicated content or truncated context.

Environment:
- Dedicated capacity (reserved GPU pool) for BrightForms Inc. in us-west-2
- Serving runtime version: v1.18.3
- Autoscaler: v0.9.1 (scale-down idle threshold 120s)
- Workers: NVIDIA A100s, driver 535.xx, CUDA 12.2
- Networking: customer VPC peering + internal NAT gateway

Steps to reproduce (customer):
1. Start long chat generation with ~2k token context and streaming enabled (SSE).
2. Request a response expected to be ~700-1200 tokens.
3. Observe tokens streaming for ~200-600 tokens, then stream silence while connection remains ESTABLISHED.
4. After ~30s silence client times out and reconnects, which resumes by starting a new generation (duplicate/truncated).

Observed logs / traces (excerpts):
- Worker stdout around stall: "[2026-03-10T15:22:31Z] kernel: triggering memory_compaction phase=post-kv-checkpoint seq=42"
- Serving runtime trace: "sse: flush-sentinel sent; downstream ack not received; stall_detected=1"
- Scheduler: "eviction_candidate: false; compaction_in_progress:true; reserved_tokens: 0.2"
- No 5xx returned to client; connection stays 200 OK. TCP shows steady bytes but no SSE payloads.

Initial hypothesis:
- A per-worker GPU memory compaction/defragmentation path (recently introduced to reduce OOMs during dynamic batch spikes) is blocking the token flush loop for multi-second windows, which for SSE looks like a silent stall to clients. The compaction code executes while a generation is in-flight causing the kernel to rebind memory and temporarily block attention kernels.

Investigation notes:
- Reproduced internally on a dedicated test pool by forcing compaction during an in-flight generation: same silent pause observed at compaction boundary.
- Tracepoints added to serving-runtime show token-emission paused during "gpu_compaction:begin" -> "gpu_compaction:end" (median pause ~4.8s, 95th ~12s).
- Autoscaler logs show no worker preemption during stalls; worker remains assigned.
- No TLS termination or proxy events correlating with the pause (TLS keepalives continue).
- Kernel JIT path triggered when switching to quantized fused-attention kernel; compaction sometimes coincides with kernel selection logic.

Mitigation performed so far:
- Temporarily disabled proactive compaction on dedicated pools for BrightForms (flags pushed to fleet: compaction.enabled=false for their pool) which reduced incidents to zero in the 6h observation window.
- Added a short client-facing warning in console for BrightForms engineers and recommended reconnect/backoff pattern as emergency fallback.

Next steps / action items:
1) Engineering (serving-runtime) to modify compaction so it yields to active token-emission loop (owner: Miguel Torres). Target: non-blocking compaction variant or scheduling compaction only when worker is idle. ETA: 2026-03-16.
2) Add server-side SSE stall detector metric and alert (observability) to catch pauses >2s during streaming (owner: Observability team). ETA: 2026-03-13.
3) Run a canary of the non-blocking compaction patch in staging with long-chat test harness for 48h (owner: QA/Eng). ETA: 2026-03-15.
4) Post-deploy: gradually re-enable compaction with throttling and monitor token-emission latencies (owner: Product/Infra).

Follow-ups requested from customer:
- Provide any client-side logs showing the exact time of stall (UTC) to match with server trace IDs.
- Confirm if they can tolerate temporary compaction disablement long-term or prefer a patch.

Resolution: ongoing. Temporary workaround applied (compaction disabled for their dedicated pool).

2) Add server-side SSE stall detector metric and alert (observability) to catch pauses >2s during streaming (owner: Observability team). ETA: 2026-03-13.
3) Run a canary of the non-blocking compaction patch in staging with long-chat test harness for 48h (owner: QA/Eng). ETA: 2026-03-15.
4) Post-deploy: gradually re-enable compaction with throttling and monitor token-emission latencies (owner: Product/Infra).

Follow-ups requested from customer:
- Provide any client-side logs showing the exact time of stall (UTC) to match with server trace IDs.
- Confirm if they can tolerate temporary compaction disablement long-term or prefer a patch.

Resolution: ongoing. Temporary workaround applied (compaction disabled for their dedicated pool).

title: Edge proxy half-close results in silent stream tail and missing final tokens for SSE sessions
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports that many SSE streams from the hosted API appear to terminate 'silently': the client receives the penultimate chunks and then the connection closes or reuses the underlying TCP connection without the expected final 'end' event or final JSON metadata. The visible symptom is truncated final tokens and missing choice/finish_reason metadata.

Impact:
- Multiple calls from NimbusAI show incomplete responses for medium-length completions (typically 800-2k tokens).
- Affects production traffic routed through our edge proxy in us-west-2.
- Customer experienced degraded UX in customer-facing chat flows (some messages appear cut off).

Environment:
- Redwood hosted API (production) behind edge proxy (Nginx-based layer) -> api-gateway -> serving-runtime (GPUs).
- Customer traffic class: dedicated pool (reserved capacity).
- Observed primarily on HTTP/1.1 keepalive connections with higher request concurrency.

Steps to reproduce (observed from customer and reproduced locally):
1) Client issues SSE generation call using chunked encoding over HTTP/1.1 (curl example provided).
2) Server emits streaming choices and final metadata chunk.
3) Under proxy connection reuse / half-close timing, the final metadata chunk is consolidated with a subsequent keepalive/newline heartbeat chunk and dropped by the proxy as an orphan fragment.
4) Client either sees connection close without end event or a truncated last choice.

Minimal repro curl (observed):
  curl -N -H "Accept: text/event-stream" 
    -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" 
    "https://api.redwood.ai/v1/streams?model=redwood-v1.2&max_tokens=1500"

Observed logs / snippets:
- Edge proxy access log (sample):
  2026-03-09T22:11:04.423Z 198.51.100.22 "POST /v1/streams HTTP/1.1" 200 2345 "-" "curl/7.85.0" "keepalive=1; reuse=1"
- Serving runtime trace (span):
  span=stream:emit chunk_id=521 status=written flush=ok
  span=stream:emit chunk_id=522 status=written flush=pending
- Customer client log (truncated final payload):
  [22:11:04.439] RECEIVED: {"delta":"...final text partial","chunk_id":521}
  [22:11:04.452] CONNECTION CLOSED (no end event)

Investigation notes so far:
- Hypothesis: Edge proxy is performing a half-close/connection reuse optimization and coalescing a tiny final chunk (final metadata or '
' heartbeat) into a previously flushed packet; due to keepalive logic or an upstream timeout, this coalesced fragment is never forwarded as a proper SSE event boundary.
- Reproduced locally behind a simplified Nginx config with HTTP/1.1 keepalive and small proxy_send_timeout (50s) when traffic is bursty. Setting proxy_buffering off reduces the rate of failures.
- No evidence of serving-runtime failing to write the final chunk; runtime traces show write attempts and flush requests. Problem appears between serving-runtime socket write and the client-observed SSE framing.
- Works reliably when forcing HTTP/2 (h2) at the edge or when client uses a fresh TCP connection per request (no keepalive).

Temporary mitigations tried and results:
- Turned off proxy buffering (proxy_buffering off) on a test edge instance -> repro rate dropped from ~12% to ~1.5%.
- Increased proxy_send_timeout and proxy_read_timeout -> minor improvement but not eliminated.
- Instructed customer to enable HTTP/2 in their client path (not viable for all due to legacy stack).
- Implemented a server-side small trailing chunk hack in a lab build that emits an explicit SSE 'end-of-stream' sentinel as a separate write (flush) and observed reliable delivery in lab.

Next steps / proposed engineering actions:
1) Short-term: Recommend edge config change for affected customers: set proxy_buffering off for SSE endpoints and ensure proxy does not coalesce tiny final chunks (document in KB). Ops to roll a canary of this config to 2 edge nodes in us-west-2.
2) Medium-term (engineering): Add a server-side safety write: always emit a dedicated terminal SSE event (e.g., 'event: _terminal
data: {"_final":true}

') as a separate flushable chunk to make end-of-stream robust to proxy coalescing. Add telemetry to count how often we needed the terminal write to be resent.
3) Long-term: Evaluate upgrading edge terminating layer to prefer HTTP/2 for streaming or hardening HTTP/1.1 chunk framing behavior (ensure transparent pass-through of tiny chunks and keepalive heartbeats).

Workarounds for customer:
- Enable HTTP/2 between client and edge if possible.
- Configure edge proxy to disable buffering for SSE endpoints (proxy_buffering off).
- Application-level: client can request a small post-stream validation token appended to payload ('append_status': true) which serving-runtime will put in the penultimate chunk to reduce reliance on a separate final chunk.

Acceptance criteria for fix:
- Repro rate for this vector drops to <0.1% for production traffic in us-west-2 across a 48h window after canary rollout.
- No increase in other streaming regressions (e.g., duplicate tokens).
- KB updated with configuration guidance and a postmortem-style incident note if customer impact is confirmed.
